applications.name: AIP Chatbot Studio
applications.description: The builder for conversational AI agents: configure system prompts, retrieval over documents and objects, and tools (Functions, Actions) the agent may use, then publish chat agents to users and applications.
applications.use_case: Create governed, ontology-aware chat assistants — a maintenance copilot that knows your machines and can file work orders via Actions.
applications.tips: Agents become genuinely useful when given ontology tools — retrieval alone is a demo, retrieval + Actions is an operational workflow.

applications.name: AIP Assist
applications.description: The platform-wide AI assistant: an LLM copilot embedded across Foundry that answers questions about the platform, explains resources, and helps generate pipelines, code, and app configurations.
applications.use_case: Accelerate every workflow — ask how to do something in Foundry, generate a Pipeline Builder transform, explain an unfamiliar dataset or repository.
applications.tips: Turn it on from day one — it is the best onboarding tool in the platform. Ask it "how do I…" before reaching for the docs.

applications.name: AIP Evals
applications.description: The evaluation framework for AI workflows: define test suites with expected outcomes, run them against Logic functions and agents across model/prompt versions, and compare results before shipping.
applications.use_case: Make LLM workflows production-grade — regression-test prompts, compare models, and quantify quality instead of eyeballing outputs.
applications.tips: Treat Evals like unit tests for AI: no Logic function or agent should ship to operations without an eval suite behind it.

applications.name: AIP Logic
applications.description: A no-code board for building LLM-powered functions: compose prompts, ontology context, tool calls, and structured outputs into testable "Logic functions" that can act on the Ontology.
applications.use_case: Build production LLM workflows — summarize objects, extract structure from documents, recommend actions — without managing models or glue code.
applications.tips: The heart of "AIP on the Ontology". Because Logic functions are just Functions, everything that can call a Function (Workshop, Automate, OSDK) can call your LLM logic.

applications.name: Modeling Objectives
applications.description: The model lifecycle manager: register models (Foundry-trained or external), evaluate and compare candidates against an objective, then release and deploy them as live endpoints or batch transforms.
applications.use_case: Productionize ML — manage staging/production releases, host inference endpoints, and wire model outputs into pipelines, Functions, and apps.
applications.tips: The MLOps backbone of Foundry: train wherever you like (Code Workspaces, externally), but deploy and govern through Modeling Objectives.

applications.name: Contour
applications.description: Point-and-click analysis on top of datasets: build "analysis paths" of filters, joins, pivots, and charts on billions of rows without writing code, and save them as dashboards.
applications.use_case: Tabular, dataset-level exploration and BI-style analysis — the everyday tool for analysts to answer questions from datasets quickly.
applications.tips: The classic first analytics tool in Foundry. Works on datasets (pre-ontology); use Quiver when you want to analyze ontology objects instead.

applications.name: Fusion
applications.description: Foundry's spreadsheet application: familiar formulas and sheets, but connected live to datasets and ontology objects, with write-back and branching instead of CSV exports.
applications.use_case: Give spreadsheet-native users (finance, planning, ops) a live, governed Excel-like surface over platform data.
applications.tips: Great adoption bridge for Excel-heavy teams — the data stays governed and refreshable instead of dying in email attachments.

applications.name: Map
applications.description: The geospatial application: layer ontology objects, imagery, and geo-data on interactive maps, with spatial and temporal filtering and analysis tools.
applications.use_case: Geospatial situational awareness and analysis — fleet positions, facility networks, territory analysis — driven by ontology objects with location properties.
applications.tips: Only relevant if your objects have geospatial properties — but indispensable when they do. Maps embed nicely into Workshop apps.

applications.name: Notepad
applications.description: Collaborative documents with live, embedded platform content: Contour charts, Quiver cards, object tables, and metrics stay up to date inside the narrative text.
applications.use_case: Write reports, runbooks, and decision documents whose numbers and charts update automatically with the data.
applications.tips: Use Notepad for anything you'd otherwise paste screenshots into a doc for — embedded analyses keep living documents honest.

applications.name: Quiver
applications.description: The object-centric analytics application: charts, time series, pivots, and interactive dashboards built directly on ontology objects and their links, with drill-through to the underlying objects.
applications.use_case: Analyze the operational world via the Ontology — e.g. plot sensor time series per Machine object, then pivot to its open work orders.
applications.tips: Think "Contour, but for objects". Quiver cards can be embedded into Workshop apps, which makes it a building block, not just an analysis tool.

applications.name: Developer Console
applications.description: The portal for building external applications with the Ontology SDK (OSDK): generates typed TypeScript/Python/Java SDKs from your ontology and manages OAuth clients, scopes, and API access.
applications.use_case: Build custom websites, mobile apps, or backend services outside Foundry that read and write the Ontology through generated, type-safe SDKs.
applications.tips: The escape hatch beyond Workshop: full developer freedom (React, mobile, services) while keeping ontology governance. OSDK is one of the fastest-evolving parts of the platform.

applications.name: Marketplace
applications.description: The packaging and distribution system (Foundry DevOps): bundle pipelines, ontologies, Workshop apps, and AIP logic into installable, upgradeable products that can be deployed across spaces and stacks.
applications.use_case: Ship a complete use case from a dev environment to production — or to another organization — as a versioned, parameterized product instead of manual copying.
applications.tips: This is "DevOps for use cases". Also where Palantir-built reference solutions are installed from — browsing it is a great way to learn solution patterns.

applications.name: Slate
applications.description: The original application builder: fully custom HTML/CSS/JavaScript apps with handlebars templating and direct query wiring. Maximum flexibility, but no ontology-native widgets and a much steeper maintenance burden.
applications.use_case: Legacy custom dashboards and apps, or rare pixel-perfect/custom-JS requirements that Workshop cannot meet. New development should default to Workshop.
applications.era: Legacy — superseded by Workshop for most uses
applications.tips: You will meet Slate apps in older deployments. Learn to maintain them if needed, but build new applications in Workshop (or OSDK for fully custom needs).

applications.name: Solution Designer
applications.description: A visual canvas for designing solution architectures: diagram the datasets, ontology entities, and applications of a use case before and while you build it, linked to the real resources.
applications.use_case: Plan and communicate the architecture of a Foundry use case — the blueprint that engineering, ontology, and app work align on.
applications.tips: Useful on multi-team builds: it keeps the target architecture visible and clickable rather than buried in slide decks.

applications.name: Workshop
applications.description: The drag-and-drop builder for operational applications on the Ontology: assemble pages from widgets (tables, charts, forms, maps), wire them to object sets, variables, Functions, and Actions — no code required.
applications.use_case: Build the apps end users actually work in every day: triage queues, planning tools, 360° views — reading from the Ontology and writing back via Actions.
applications.tips: Workshop is where the Ontology pays off — most Foundry value reaches end users through a Workshop app. The single most important app-building skill on the platform.

applications.name: Code Repositories
applications.description: A full web-based development environment with Git version control for writing data transformations in Python (PySpark), Java, or SQL, as well as TypeScript/Python Functions — with CI checks, branching, and pull requests.
applications.use_case: Write code-based data transforms when logic outgrows Pipeline Builder, and author Functions that power Actions and application logic.
applications.tips: The pro-code counterpart of Pipeline Builder. If you know Git and PySpark you will feel at home. Used both for heavy data transforms and for authoring Functions.

applications.name: Code Workbook
applications.description: The original notebook-style analysis and transformation tool, mixing Python/R/SQL nodes in a visual graph. Still encountered on many stacks but no longer the recommended option.
applications.use_case: Legacy exploratory analysis and lightweight transforms; on new projects prefer Code Workspaces (analysis) and Pipeline Builder / Code Repositories (pipelines).
applications.tips: You will find plenty of existing Workbooks in older projects, so it's worth being able to read them — but don't start new work here.

applications.name: Code Workspaces
applications.description: Hosted JupyterLab and RStudio environments running inside Foundry, with direct access to datasets and the Ontology — for exploratory data science and model development.
applications.use_case: Do iterative, notebook-style data science (EDA, feature engineering, model training) in familiar tools without leaving the platform.
applications.tips: The modern home for data scientists, replacing most Code Workbook use cases. Models trained here are submitted to Modeling Objectives for productionization.

applications.name: Data Connection
applications.description: The gateway between Foundry and the outside world. Provides 200+ connectors (JDBC, SAP, Salesforce, S3, REST APIs, ...) plus on-premise agents to sync external systems into Foundry datasets, in batch or streaming mode.
applications.use_case: Set up syncs from source systems (databases, ERPs, cloud storage, APIs) into raw Foundry datasets, and export data back out to external systems.
applications.tips: You usually consume data someone else connected before you set up your own sync. Understand sources, agents, and sync schedules before diving into connector specifics.

applications.name: Data Lineage
applications.description: An interactive graph of how datasets, transforms, models, and ontology objects depend on each other across the platform, with build status, schedules, and health overlays.
applications.use_case: Trace where data comes from and where it flows to, run impact analysis before changing a dataset, and debug pipeline failures end-to-end.
applications.tips: Open Data Lineage whenever you inherit an unfamiliar pipeline — it answers "where does this column come from?" faster than reading code.

applications.name: Pipeline Builder
applications.description: A visual, point-and-click application for building production data pipelines — batch or streaming — with transforms, joins, and cleaning steps, all version-controlled with branching and proposals.
applications.use_case: Build and deploy data transformation pipelines without writing code; the recommended starting point for most data integration work, including ontology hydration.
applications.tips: The modern default for pipelines. Start here before Code Repositories — most pipelines never need custom code, and Pipeline Builder outputs can hydrate the Ontology directly.

applications.name: Approvals
applications.description: The review workflow system: changes to governed resources — ontology proposals, marketplace installations, checkout requests — go through structured propose/review/approve flows with full audit trail.
applications.use_case: Gate sensitive changes behind peer review, e.g. ontology schema changes proposed by builders and approved by ontology owners.
applications.tips: Expect to meet Approvals the first time you propose an ontology change on a governed stack — it is Git-style review applied to platform configuration.

applications.name: Builds & Schedules
applications.description: The execution layer for all computation: every transform run is a job inside a build. Inspect running and historical builds, read logs, and define schedules that keep pipelines fresh (time- or event-triggered).
applications.use_case: Monitor and debug pipeline executions, and schedule when datasets rebuild — the operational heartbeat of data integration.
applications.tips: When a pipeline "doesn't work", the answer is almost always in the build logs. Learn to read a build graph early — it pays off daily.

applications.name: Checkpoints
applications.description: A governance mechanism that interrupts sensitive operations — exports, marking overrides, large queries — to require user justifications, which are recorded for audit.
applications.use_case: Enforce "stop and justify" moments on risky actions so security teams get an auditable trail of who did what and why.
applications.tips: Mostly configured by platform administrators — as a builder you'll experience checkpoints rather than create them.

applications.name: Control Panel
applications.description: The administration console: manage organizations, users and groups (via Multipass identity), markings and classification, enrollment settings, resource quotas, and platform-wide feature configuration.
applications.use_case: Administer the Foundry enrollment — identity providers, security markings, org boundaries, and which features and models are enabled.
applications.tips: Admin territory. Builders mainly need to know it exists and that markings/permissions configured here override everything downstream.

applications.name: Data Health
applications.description: The data quality application: attach health checks to datasets — freshness, schema, row counts, null ratios, custom expectations — and aggregate their status into monitored check groups.
applications.use_case: Catch data quality problems before consumers do: alert when a critical dataset is late, shrinks unexpectedly, or breaks schema.
applications.tips: Production pipelines without health checks are incidents waiting to happen. Add freshness + row count checks to every dataset that feeds the Ontology.

applications.name: Projects & Files
applications.description: The filesystem of Foundry (built on Compass): every resource — dataset, pipeline, app, ontology artifact — lives in folders within Projects, which are the primary unit of organization and access control.
applications.use_case: Navigate and organize all platform resources, and manage who can see and edit what through project roles and permissions.
applications.tips: Literally the first thing you see in Foundry. Understand Projects → folders → resources and how roles cascade before creating anything.

applications.name: Automate
applications.description: The platform's automation engine (evolved from Object Monitoring): watch objects, metrics, or schedules for conditions and trigger effects — notifications, Actions, Logic functions — when they fire.
applications.use_case: Turn passive data into proactive operations: "notify the planner when stock drops below threshold", "auto-apply this Action when a job fails".
applications.tips: The bridge from dashboards (humans watch data) to operations (the platform watches data). Pairs naturally with Actions and AIP Logic.

applications.name: Machinery
applications.description: A process-orchestration application that defines stateful, multi-step operational processes (state machines) over ontology objects, coordinating humans and automation through each stage.
applications.use_case: Model long-running business processes — approvals chains, maintenance workflows, order lifecycles — with explicit states, transitions, and task assignment.
applications.tips: Niche but powerful: reach for Machinery when a workflow has real states and handoffs, not just notifications.

applications.name: Object Explorer
applications.description: A search-and-browse interface over all ontology objects: filter, pivot across links, build and save object sets, and view individual objects with their properties and history.
applications.use_case: Explore the ontology like a search engine — find objects, filter them into object sets, and hand those sets to Quiver, Workshop, or Actions.
applications.tips: The fastest way to "feel" what the Ontology is. Object sets you build here are reusable across the whole platform.

applications.name: Ontology Manager
applications.description: The control center for the Ontology — Foundry's semantic layer that turns datasets into Object Types (e.g. Customer, Flight, Machine) with properties, Links between them, and Actions that write changes back.
applications.use_case: Define and maintain object types, link types, action types, and their permissions — the shared "digital twin" vocabulary every operational app is built on.
applications.tips: The Ontology is THE central concept of Foundry — it is what separates Foundry from a classic data platform. Understand object types, links, and actions before touching Workshop or AIP.

applications.name: Vertex
applications.description: A graph-and-scenario application over the Ontology: visualize networks of linked objects as an interactive "digital twin" and simulate what-if scenarios on top of them.
applications.use_case: Model and compare scenarios on connected systems — supply chains, networks, production plans — before committing changes back through Actions.
applications.tips: Powerful for supply-chain and network use cases; only worth learning once your ontology is rich in links.

applications.name: Artifacts
applications.description: A repository for storing and versioning code artifacts — Python/conda packages, Maven and npm libraries, and container images — that repositories and workspaces build against.
applications.use_case: Publish an internal Python package once and depend on it from many repositories; pin library versions for reproducible builds.
applications.tips: Most teams never open Artifacts directly — it sits behind Code Repositories. Learn it when you need shared internal libraries.

applications.name: Compute Modules
applications.description: Run custom containerized logic and long-running services inside Foundry, exposing functions backed by your own Docker images.
applications.use_case: Host bespoke compute — a custom model server, a heavy transformation, or an external API wrapper — and call it as a governed function.
applications.tips: Reach for Compute Modules only when Pipeline Builder and Code Repositories can't express the logic you need.

applications.name: HyperAuto
applications.description: Automatically generates and maintains pipelines from enterprise source systems (such as SAP), turning raw extracts into clean, ontology-ready datasets.
applications.use_case: Stand up a governed SAP integration in days instead of months by letting HyperAuto build the extraction and cleaning pipelines for you.
applications.tips: Hugely valuable for big ERP sources — but overkill for a handful of CSVs.

applications.name: Jupyter
applications.description: A hosted JupyterLab environment — a flavor of Code Workspaces — for interactive Python notebook analysis directly on Foundry data.
applications.use_case: Explore datasets ad hoc, prototype models, and run scratch analysis in familiar notebooks without moving data off-platform.
applications.tips: Great for exploration; promote anything you want to keep into a Code Repository so it is scheduled and versioned.

applications.name: RStudio
applications.description: A hosted RStudio environment — a flavor of Code Workspaces — for statistical analysis and modeling in R on Foundry data.
applications.use_case: Run R-based statistics, econometrics, or bioinformatics workflows against governed datasets.
applications.tips: Pick RStudio when your team already lives in R; otherwise the Python tooling is more deeply integrated.

applications.name: VS Code
applications.description: A hosted Visual Studio Code workspace — a flavor of Code Workspaces — for full-featured development against Foundry resources.
applications.use_case: Develop with the editor you already know, with extensions and a terminal, while staying inside Foundry's governance.
applications.tips: The most flexible workspace flavor — good when you want a real IDE rather than a notebook.

applications.name: Time Series Catalog
applications.description: Registers, organizes, and serves high-volume time series data so it can be charted, attached to objects, and used in modeling.
applications.use_case: Manage millions of sensor or market series and expose them efficiently to Quiver, the ontology, and models.
applications.tips: Essential for IoT and sensor-heavy workflows; pair it with Quiver for visualization.

applications.name: Value Types
applications.description: Define reusable semantic value types (such as email, currency, or geohash) that constrain and standardize property values across the ontology.
applications.use_case: Guarantee that every 'email' property in the ontology is validated and rendered consistently, wherever it appears.
applications.tips: Define value types early — retrofitting them onto a large ontology is far more work.

applications.name: Foundry Rules
applications.description: Define and run business rules and decision logic against ontology objects to classify, flag, or trigger actions.
applications.use_case: Encode policy — eligibility checks, thresholds, routing rules — once and apply it consistently across objects and workflows.
applications.tips: Keep rules close to the ontology so the logic stays explainable and auditable.

applications.name: Insight
applications.description: A lightweight reporting tool for assembling charts, tables, and narrative into shareable analytical reports.
applications.use_case: Produce a recurring operational report or a polished one-off summary for stakeholders without building a full application.
applications.tips: Use Insight for read-only reporting; reach for Workshop when users need to take action.

applications.name: Carbon Workspace
applications.description: A customizable workspace shell that brings objects, applications, and pages from across Foundry into a single tailored homepage.
applications.use_case: Give a team one curated landing page that stitches together the Workshop apps, objects, and links they use every day.
applications.tips: Carbon is the 'front door' for end users — set one up so people don't have to hunt across the platform.

applications.name: Custom Widgets
applications.description: Build and publish custom React widgets that extend Workshop with bespoke UI components.
applications.use_case: Add a specialized visualization or interaction that Workshop's built-in widgets can't provide.
applications.tips: Only build a custom widget once you've confirmed no built-in widget fits — they carry maintenance cost.

applications.name: AIP Analyst
applications.description: An AI assistant for analytical exploration that answers questions over your ontology and data and helps build the analysis to support them.
applications.use_case: Ask a question in plain language and get back charts, object sets, and explanations grounded in your data.
applications.tips: Treat its output as a starting point — verify the underlying object sets before acting on conclusions.

applications.name: Pilot
applications.description: An autonomous AIP agent that plans and executes multi-step tasks across Foundry, calling tools and actions to reach a goal.
applications.use_case: Hand off a higher-level objective and let Pilot chain together the queries, actions, and steps needed to complete it.
applications.tips: Beta — keep a human in the loop and scope its available tools carefully.

applications.name: AIP Threads
applications.description: Collaborative, shareable AI chat threads grounded in your Foundry context for ongoing analysis and Q&A.
applications.use_case: Keep a persistent, shareable conversation with the AI about a workflow, so teammates can pick up the same context.
applications.tips: Beta — use threads to share reasoning with teammates rather than re-asking the same questions.

applications.name: AIP Document Intelligence
applications.description: Extracts structured data from unstructured documents — PDFs, scans, forms — using AI, turning them into ontology objects.
applications.use_case: Convert a backlog of contracts or invoices into queryable objects you can analyze and act on.
applications.tips: Spend time on the extraction schema up front — clean structure here pays off everywhere downstream.

applications.name: Model Catalog
applications.description: Browse, register, and manage machine learning models — foundation models and your own — available for use across the platform.
applications.use_case: Find an approved model, see how it's governed, and reuse it instead of re-importing or retraining.
applications.tips: Check the catalog before importing a new model — the one you need may already be registered and governed.

applications.name: Model Studio
applications.description: An interactive environment for building, training, and evaluating models without leaving the platform.
applications.use_case: Iterate on a model — train, compare runs, and evaluate — then publish it to the Model Catalog.
applications.tips: Promote promising experiments into Modeling Objectives so deployment stays governed.

applications.name: AI FDE
applications.description: An AI 'forward deployed engineer' that builds end-to-end workflows — pipelines, ontology, and apps — from a high-level goal.
applications.use_case: Bootstrap a whole workflow from a description, then refine the generated pieces by hand.
applications.tips: Best as an accelerator: review and harden everything it generates before relying on it.

applications.name: Resource Management
applications.description: Manage and monitor compute and storage resource usage across the platform — view consumption, set quotas, and track spend.
applications.use_case: Find which projects and pipelines consume the most compute, set usage limits, and control platform cost.
applications.tips: Check resource usage before scaling a pipeline up — it's the fastest way to catch runaway cost.

applications.name: Upgrade Assistant
applications.description: Helps move resources and installed products to new versions safely, surfacing what will change and what needs attention.
applications.use_case: Roll a fleet of resources forward to a new version with visibility into breaking changes.
applications.tips: Run it in a branch first so you can review the impact before upgrading production.

applications.name: DevOps
applications.description: Build, version, and operate installable products — package resources into products, manage releases, and monitor installations across environments.
applications.use_case: Ship a repeatable solution to many tenants or environments and keep track of every installation's version and health.
applications.tips: Lean on DevOps once a solution needs to be deployed more than once — it makes Marketplace products operable at scale.

applications.name: Global Branching
applications.description: Create and manage branches that span many resources at once, enabling coordinated, isolated development across an entire workflow.
applications.use_case: Develop a cross-cutting change — pipelines, ontology, and apps together — on one branch, then merge it as a unit.
applications.tips: Use a global branch when a change touches several resources that must move together.

applications.name: Workflow Lineage
applications.description: Visualizes end-to-end lineage across an entire workflow — from source data through pipelines, ontology, and applications.
applications.use_case: Trace how a number on a dashboard traces all the way back to its source systems, across resource types.
applications.tips: Pair it with Data Lineage: Data Lineage covers datasets, Workflow Lineage covers the whole workflow.

applications.name: Cipher
applications.description: Applies tokenization and encryption to sensitive columns, controlling who can decrypt data through governed Cipher channels.
applications.use_case: Keep PII or secrets protected at rest while still allowing authorized users to compute on decrypted values.
applications.tips: Design Cipher channels with security up front — they're central to how sensitive data flows through the platform.

applications.name: Sensitive Data Scanner
applications.description: Automatically scans datasets to detect and classify sensitive data (PII, secrets) and apply protective markings.
applications.use_case: Catch unmarked PII before it spreads, and drive markings or Cipher protection automatically.
applications.tips: Run the scanner across new data sources early — it's far cheaper than remediating leaks later.

applications.name: Issues
applications.description: Track, discuss, and resolve issues on resources and objects — a built-in collaboration and ticketing surface across Foundry.
applications.use_case: Flag a data-quality problem or a bug right on the resource and keep the conversation next to the work.
applications.tips: Use Issues instead of email threads — keeping the discussion on the resource preserves context.

applications.name: Linter
applications.description: Automatically checks resources against best-practice and health rules, surfacing problems and recommended fixes.
applications.use_case: Catch anti-patterns in pipelines and repositories before they cause failures or tech debt.
applications.tips: Treat lint warnings like compiler warnings — clear them early to keep workflows healthy.

applications.name: Examples (Build with AIP)
applications.description: A gallery of example workflows and applications that demonstrate how to build with AIP and the rest of the platform.
applications.use_case: Find a worked example close to what you want to build and adapt it instead of starting from scratch.
applications.tips: Skim the examples before starting a new build — you'll often find a pattern to copy.

applications.name: Training
applications.description: Structured, hands-on courses that teach Foundry skills, from beginner onboarding to advanced workflows.
applications.use_case: Onboard a new team member with a guided curriculum rather than ad hoc shoulder-surfing.
applications.tips: Send new users to Training first — a few hours here saves days of confusion later.

applications.name: Walkthroughs
applications.description: Guided, in-product interactive tutorials that walk you through building real workflows step by step.
applications.use_case: Learn an app by doing — follow an interactive walkthrough inside the product instead of reading docs alone.
applications.tips: Great for the first time you touch an app; pair with the docs for depth.

applications.name: Foundry Platform Documentation
applications.description: The official Foundry product documentation — reference, how-to guides, and concept explanations for every application.
applications.use_case: Look up exactly how a feature works, with authoritative reference and examples.
applications.tips: When AIP Assist and the docs disagree, trust the docs — they're the source of truth.

links.description: Synced raw datasets are the typical inputs of Pipeline Builder pipelines.
Synced datasets are read by code-based transforms.
Clean pipeline outputs back (hydrate) ontology object types.
Code-based transform outputs back ontology object types.
Pipeline output datasets are analyzed in Contour.
Code Workspaces is the modern replacement for most Code Workbook usage.
Pipeline Builder replaces Workbook-built production transforms.
Models trained in notebooks are submitted to Modeling Objectives.
Model adapters and training code publish models to objectives.
Lineage visualizes pipeline dependency graphs and build status.
Lineage traces code-based transforms across datasets.
Every pipeline deployment runs as builds with logs and schedules.
Transform jobs run as builds; schedules keep outputs fresh.
Health checks watch freshness, size, and schema of pipeline outputs.
Health status overlays on the lineage graph for at-a-glance triage.
Failing health checks can trigger automations and notifications.
Object Explorer searches and browses the object types defined here.
Workshop apps are built on object sets, links, and Actions.
Quiver analyzes ontology objects, links, and time series.
Vertex graphs and scenarios run over linked ontology objects.
Map renders objects with geospatial properties as live layers.
Fusion sheets can read and write ontology objects.
Automations watch object conditions and trigger ontology Actions.
Process states and transitions are defined over ontology objects.
Logic functions receive ontology context and act through Actions.
Agents retrieve objects and call Actions as governed tools.
OSDK generates typed SDKs from the ontology definition.
Object sets built in Explorer open directly in Quiver for analysis.
Quiver cards and dashboards embed inside Workshop modules.
Maps embed as widgets inside Workshop applications.
Contour charts and tables embed live into Notepad documents.
Quiver visualizations embed live into Notepad documents.
Workshop is the recommended replacement for most Slate apps.
Assist generates and explains pipeline transforms.
Assist helps configure widgets and app logic.
Assist builds analysis paths from natural-language questions.
Assist explains and generates transform code.
Logic functions serve as tools that agents can invoke.
Automations call Logic functions to add AI to triggered workflows.
Eval suites regression-test Logic functions across versions.
Eval suites score agent behavior before publishing.
Published agents embed as chat widgets in Workshop apps.
Ontology resources live in projects; project roles gate who edits them.
Pipelines and their datasets are organized and permissioned via projects.
Org structure, markings, and identity configured here scope all projects.
Administrators configure checkpoint policies platform-wide.
Ontology proposals are reviewed and approved before taking effect.
Workshop apps ship inside installable Marketplace products.
Ontology definitions are packaged and deployed as products.
Pipelines are bundled into products for cross-stack deployment.
Architectures designed here are realized and shipped via Marketplace.
Hosts the libraries and packages repositories build against.
Supplies versioned packages and images to workspaces.
Repositories define and publish compute modules.
Host custom logic that backs ontology Actions and functions.
Source-system syncs feed HyperAuto's automated pipeline generation.
Produces clean, ontology-ready datasets.
A Code Workspaces environment flavor.
A Code Workspaces environment flavor.
A Code Workspaces environment flavor.
Pipelines populate registered time series.
Serves time series into Quiver for charting.
Constrain and standardize ontology property values.
Rules evaluate against ontology objects.
Rule outcomes can trigger automations.
Reports are built on ontology objects.
A lighter-weight reporting companion to Quiver.
Workshop apps surface inside a Carbon workspace.
Object views can be pinned into a Carbon homepage.
Custom React widgets embed into Workshop pages.
Answers questions grounded in ontology objects.
Helps build analytical explorations.
Pilot retrieves objects and calls Actions as governed tools.
Logic functions serve as tools Pilot can invoke.
Persistent, shareable threads alongside the inline assistant.
Provides documents for AI extraction.
